The Ghosts in the Machine
For Al and Bea
October 31, 2015
It’s so noisy here I can hardly think. How do they expect me to get any work done? I have stuff
to do! Lulabelle and Thor (not their real names - names have been changed to protect the
guilty) are conducting a thinly veiled mating dance at Thor’s cubicle. Lulabelle’s goose-cackle of
a laugh honks in response to each of his hearty, masculine inanities. Why don’t they just sneak
off to the server room and DO IT already? Perhaps someone could throw a bucket of cold water
on them? Those two marketing interns over there, I forget their names, Frick and Frack or Mick
and Mack, are gabbling about their college hockey teams. How the hell does anybody get any
work done around here?
Fortunately, I have a pair of noise-canceling headphones. (How I came to possess these
marvels of electro-acoustical art and science is a story for another day!) The inventor of

Fortunately, I have a pair of noise-canceling headphones. (How I came to possess these
marvels of electro-acoustical art and science is a story for another day!) The inventor of
noise-canceling headphones is a benefactor of humanity, in my opinion. On a par with Fleming,
the penicillin guy and whoever it was who came up with the grilled cheese sandwich. It’s so
NOISY around here. I don my wonderful noise canceling headphones. They are substantial.
My neck bows beneath their weight. I switch them on. The babble of a room full of twenty
people, all determined to abstain from productive activity as if it were a vicious and disreputable
habit, is reduced to a tinny buzz like the sound of bees in a bottle. I use music to mask the
noise of unruly coworkers the way Louis XIV’s unwashed courtiers used perfume to cover the
stink of their fellow unwashed courtiers. Also, I like to listen to music while I work. I find a

noise of unruly coworkers the way Louis XIV’s unwashed courtiers used perfume to cover the
stink of their fellow unwashed courtiers. Also, I like to listen to music while I work. I find a
Youtube of Glenn Gould doing the Goldberg Variations, the 1981 version, the slow one where
he sort of sings along. The tinny buzz is not completely eradicated by an aural cloud of soulful
polyphony. It’s better, though. Now I can begin.
Something is making a lot of noise up on the roof. Maybe it’s a heater or an air conditioner. Or
perhaps it’s a piece of equipment in one of the other labs in the building, a huge fan or
compressor or some other large rumbly thing. It’s right over my head and makes this
thrum-rumbagumbarooo-thaTUMMtumtumtum sound that never ceases, not even for a moment.
The compressor of Damocles. My work is difficult. I need to concentrate. It’s a general AI
module - AI for Artificial Intelligence. People have been working on AI since before Minsky in

The compressor of Damocles. My work is difficult. I need to concentrate. It’s a general AI
module - AI for Artificial Intelligence. People have been working on AI since before Minsky in
the sixties. Nobody’s gotten anywhere to speak of. That’s a good one - to speak of. You
evaluate how good an AI is by giving it the Turing Test. Named after Alan Turing, the gay
mathematician who helped win World War Two, invented computers and killed himself by eating
an apple he’d poisoned - Snow White to his own Wicked Stepmother. The headphones don’t
drown out the racket from above. Its driving me nuts. The Turing Test is just this: someone who
doesn’t know whether he or she is talking to a person or an Artificial Intelligence has a
conversation with it. If, at the end of the conversation, the human believes that he or she has

been conversing with another human, the AI has passed the test. Easy-peasy, right? Well,
maybe not. A lot of smart people have been failing at this game for the past fifty years. It’s like
controlled nuclear fusion or fully autonomous cars: going to change everything and always just
around the corner. I give myself to the music, sinking into that semi-hypnotized state of relaxed
but focused attention from which all my best ideas seem to emerge, almost effortlessly and
unbidden. Quite a few of them, anyway. Some.
With the exterior noises attenuated to the point that they can be ignored, The
boom-ba-boom-ba-BOOM of my heart is loud. It’s a crude sound, like the bass drum in an
oompah band. The blood rushing through my arteries roars continuously, noisy as water
gushing from a burst main.
My stomach makes its usual, tragicomic racket, you know how it
goes: gurgling, burbling, trombone farty sounds that must be audible as far away as Central

gushing from a burst main.
My stomach makes its usual, tragicomic racket, you know how it
goes: gurgling, burbling, trombone farty sounds that must be audible as far away as Central
Square (the office is down by the Charles). I think I can hear my hair growing, at least around
my ears. An unsettling kind of slithering rustle. My thoughts themselves are not silent. The
sound they make as I think them is that of a myriad of tiny paws, skittering across a pantry floor
long after midnight. Someplace far away, a car alarm serenades nobody but me.
Just as I begin to despair of ever getting down to work, my attention is captured - no, rescued -
by the Quodlibet - da BUM ba da da DA dada dum da DUM da dum da dee diddly um - I’m
THERE.
In the zone. Ready to work. Relaxed. Focused. I open a command window on the
computer and ssh into the cloud. My module requires a lot of computational resources: so
much processing power, memory, storage, etc. that I have to run it “in the cloud.” This really

computer and ssh into the cloud. My module requires a lot of computational resources: so
much processing power, memory, storage, etc. that I have to run it “in the cloud.” This really
means that I run the program on a network of powerful machines located who knows where,
owned, operated and maintained by Orinoco.com, the enormous corporation that has come to
dominate the online toothpaste market and is worth several times the GDP of Burkina Faso. I
rent a minute fraction of their unused computing capacity and do my work on them, or it, or
however you say it. In the cloud.
The idea behind my AI module is not all that complicated, in principle. It’s based on a
conceptual dingus called a “Perceptron”. Perceptrons have been around forever but never
really took off. In the fifties, Rosenblatt, their inventor, claimed that they would be "the embryo
of an electronic computer that [the Navy] expects will be able to walk, talk, see, write, reproduce

really took off. In the fifties, Rosenblatt, their inventor, claimed that they would be "the embryo
of an electronic computer that [the Navy] expects will be able to walk, talk, see, write, reproduce
itself and be conscious of its existence." So far, this hasn’t happened but it’s early days yet,
humans, so don’t give up hope. I’ve modeled the mind as an ensemble of loosely coupled,
lightweight perceptrons. I call them “perceptrini”. A perceptrino is a tiny perceptron, able to
keep only a single thought in its little head, or to make only one simple decision. The mind,
according to my theory at least, is an emergent property of this swarm of barely intelligent
agents. Technical Personhood occurs when the perceptrini develop an affinity for one another,
integrate into a coherent entity and learn to function cooperatively. At least that’s the theory.
My AI module is called Alvin. It’s named after my father, who passed away a few years ago.

integrate into a coherent entity and learn to function cooperatively. At least that’s the theory.
My AI module is called Alvin. It’s named after my father, who passed away a few years ago.
Also, the letters A and I look a lot like ‘AI’, my dad’s name. It’s kind of a pun that only I can get.
A private little joke. Most people think I named it after the leader of an all-rodent vocal group

that was popular a few years back.
“Passed away” is a nasty, mealy-mouthed euphemism. He
died. My father died. He kicked the bucket. Punched the clock. Roped him a steer in that old
last roundup. Whatever. He is not around anymore. He left quite a bit of writing though:
journals covering the final twenty years of his life, five half finished novels (one mystery, three
science fiction and a cowboy story, all focusing on transexual themes - I never knew) two
finished novels (one heavily influenced by The Christine Jorgenson Story) and Tiresias!, a
musical about a man who becomes a woman, falls in love with the Panchen Lama (tragically
unrequited), leaves his wife of forty some odd years and opens an autobody shop and
laundromat in Hackensack, NJ, where he/she dispenses wise but cryptic advice to customers of
all genders at both establishments while folding laundry and straightening fenders. I also have

laundromat in Hackensack, NJ, where he/she dispenses wise but cryptic advice to customers of
all genders at both establishments while folding laundry and straightening fenders. I also have
a few letters he wrote me when I was in Oregon at college - no trans subjects in them, mostly
unsolicited advice he could have co-authored with Polonius. I had all these texts digitized. A
messy job. Most of the material was sticky with tobacco residue, coffee stains, spilled gin and
tonic, cryptic bodily fluids, etc. Some of it was in the form of early computer printout - fading dot
matrix print on brittle yellowed fanfold paper. A lot of it was written in Al’s nearly indecipherable
scrawl in bound, ruled notebooks with black and white, mottled cardboard covers or on scraps
of paper I rescued when I cleaned out his office after he died. I found an outfit in India that
would digitize all of it for short(ish) money. I shipped the texts off to India and settled down to
wait. A month passed, then two.

would digitize all of it for short(ish) money. I shipped the texts off to India and settled down to
wait. A month passed, then two.
At last I received a text from them:
Hello Mr. Peter.
Here is a link to download your digitized
text.
We have returned your originals to you by parcel post.
You will be receiving now very soon next year.
Algosoft
appreciates to be doing business with you.
Click Here To
Download. The author of these texts was an odd person.
I clicked and waited as thirty seven gigabytes of text bounced router-to-router over
low-bandwidth cables from Chennai, India to Cambridge. That was last night.
Today, I uploaded the digitized texts to Alvin. Then I invoked Al’s parse function and waited.
Parsing takes a long time if a corpus is large, or if the semantic content of the material has a
high degree of indeterminacy, polysemy or any of the five other common flavors of ambiguity.
The machine builds trees of possible semantic hierarchies, ‘meanings’ you could call them, then

high degree of indeterminacy, polysemy or any of the five other common flavors of ambiguity.
The machine builds trees of possible semantic hierarchies, ‘meanings’ you could call them, then
arranges the trees into “forests”. It then creates a network of “paths” through the forests,
connecting the trees. Finally, “Superhighways” connect all the forests into a fully connected web
of webs of all possible semantic relationships that can be inferred from the text. I call the space
in which this hyperweb is created a “noösphere” Once the parse is complete, a swarm of
perceptrini is introduced into the noösphere. If all goes according to plan, the perceptrini
comprising the Al module will assimilate the semantic content of the metaforest, form elective
affinities with each other, self-assemble into a loosely coupled entity, achieve Technical
Personhood and become conscious, whatever THAT means.

After uploading the text and invoking the parse function, there wasn’t much to do but wait and
watch the progress bar as it crawled pixel by pixel across the command window. Fifteen
minutes passed, then twenty and a half hour. The bar was only at 37%. I left for lunch. I usually
take lunch at Il Cucchiaio di Grasso, a nice little Italian bistro not too far away in Cambridgeport.
Joe and Maria, the owners, seemed happy to see me. Maria showed me to my usual table at
the back of the room, facing the door and with a good view out all the windows. Il Cucchiaio di
Grasso is off Magazine Street, not too far from Dana Park. When I lived in the neighborhood,
we called it “dogshit park” because that’s where all the dogs went to relieve themselves. The
city has cleaned it up, now that the price of real estate in the neighborhood has entered the
Kuiper belt. Joe comes by to take my order. “I’ll have the usual, Joe” I say. “We got some great

city has cleaned it up, now that the price of real estate in the neighborhood has entered the
Kuiper belt. Joe comes by to take my order. “I’ll have the usual, Joe” I say. “We got some great
specials today. You wanna hear about ‘em?” he asks me. “Sure Joe. Tell me about the
specials.” “OK. So we got Seppie con rospo e misti verdi. It’s a.. a…” - he seems at a loss for
words - “It’s a seafood dish with greens and stuff. Maybe some kinda meat too. You’ll like it.
Maria she made it last night. Also, we got Ravioli di roba disgustosa dentro. It’s a ravioli with
some… you know… I dunno… some... ...it’s good. It’s delicious. Maria, her mom she makes it
once in awhile.” “I think I’ll just have the usual today Joe.” “OK. Maria, she’ll bring it right out.”
“Thanks.”
After lunch, I stroll through the rose garden in dogshit park. I take my time walking back along
the river. What was all that transexual stuff about? Was my dad a closeted gay man? He was

“Thanks.”
After lunch, I stroll through the rose garden in dogshit park. I take my time walking back along
the river. What was all that transexual stuff about? Was my dad a closeted gay man? He was
so butch! A tough guy. Had been trained as a Ranger in the Army. Didn’t take any shit from
anybody ever. When we were kids he read Homer to us, The Iliad and The Odyssey in a kid’s
version with a lot of pictures. He seemed excited by the part where Odysseus meets Tiresias in
the underworld. Of course, ox-eyed Hera did turn Tiresias into a woman and he/she (my
daughter would have me say ‘they’ but I can’t bring myself to do it) lived as a prostitute, married
and had children before becoming a man again after seven years. In the underworld, after
Odysseus talks with Tiresias he tries to embrace Antiklea, his mother. She drifts through his
arms like smoke since she is dead and lives in the underworld. This was how my mom seemed

Odysseus talks with Tiresias he tries to embrace Antiklea, his mother. She drifts through his
arms like smoke since she is dead and lives in the underworld. This was how my mom seemed
to me after Al died: insubstantial, not wholly alive and in this world. We could talk but she wasn’t
really there. Bea and Al were married sixty eight years. She survived for another two years
after he died, but she wasn’t really living. All these memories. So strange that people can be
so alive and then just vanish. It doesn’t seem right, like there must be some kind of mix-up
someplace.
Just past the Riverside Boat Club the path along the Charles gets narrow. I fail to note a very
large goose standing erect in the middle of the path, hissing at me, his wings spread wide. I
also don’t notice several dozen slightly smaller geese down the path beyond this first goose,
who is probably the Head Goose, the Maximum Leader, Goose Numero Uno, The Goose of

also don’t notice several dozen slightly smaller geese down the path beyond this first goose,
who is probably the Head Goose, the Maximum Leader, Goose Numero Uno, The Goose of
Geese, etc. He was likely not elected to that post, but came into it because he’s the baddest
motherfucking goose in 02139. Absorbed in my ruminations, I don’t see this big bird or heed his
attempts to dissuade me from disturbing his constituents. He flies up in my face. Startled out of
oblivion, I turn and run. I never was much of a runner. I’m old. I injured my leg in a motorcycle
accident years ago. I had polio as a child and used that as an excuse to skip gym class ever

afterwards, I just plain don’t like running. Never did. But motivated by this, this creature, I
surprise myself to discover how extremely fast I can move. The bird flies after me, pecking at
my head, neck and shoulders, making the occasional humiliating and painful stab at my
buttocks. The goose’s peck feels like being hit by a club. Finally, we get far enough away from
the other birds that he decides I am no longer an immediate threat to his flock. The goose lands
in front of me, wings extended to their full six foot span. I stop suddenly and windmill my arms
backwards to keep from falling on my face. We confront one another, fight or flight reflexes fully
engaged. Slowly, he advances on me, hissing and muttering mean things in Goose. As he
approaches me, I step backwards, stumbling off the path and into Memorial Drive. An
enormous SUV thunders past, swerves around me, horn blaring, wheels squealing like a herd of

approaches me, I step backwards, stumbling off the path and into Memorial Drive. An
enormous SUV thunders past, swerves around me, horn blaring, wheels squealing like a herd of
tormented swine, scaring the crap out of both me and the goose, who flies away, his answering
honks creating a call-and-response antiphonal cacophony. I wait for a break in the traffic, cross
Memorial Drive and return to the office, shaken.
The office feels abandoned. Nobody home. Most likely they are all in a group meeting. I
stopped going to group meetings a while ago. A few months now, maybe a year. Fat waste of
time, meetings. First, The Big One inflates his mighty vocal sacs and croaks an important
sounding croak. Then a few of the peepers peep back and forth. One of the crickets chirps and
suddenly the whole pond’s croaking and peeping and chirping away, as if any of it mattered a
damn.
A tale told to an idiot, full of sound and fury, something something something, as Hamlet

suddenly the whole pond’s croaking and peeping and chirping away, as if any of it mattered a
damn.
A tale told to an idiot, full of sound and fury, something something something, as Hamlet
said. Nobody’s around. Good. I slide gingerly into my swivel chair (I’m probably all black and
blue from the goose attack) and log onto the computer. The progress bar is at 97%. Seven
more minutes. I sit staring at the ceiling, a grid of cardboard squares, interrupted at regular
intervals by panels of dim and flickering fluorescent tubes. After a while, the progress bar hits
100%. The command window prints
waldo>Parse Complete. Populate noösphere? (y/N)
Waldo is the name of my computer. Waldo was also the name of my dog when I was in college.
He was an intelligent and charismatic golden retriever. Some people found him to be a trifle
conceited. In my opinion, his many sterling qualities amply justified his unquestionably high

He was an intelligent and charismatic golden retriever. Some people found him to be a trifle
conceited. In my opinion, his many sterling qualities amply justified his unquestionably high
self-regard. I fall into a reverie, reminiscing about Waldo - how wise and good he was, how he’d
jump into icy water and swim to retrieve a stick or ball that I’d thrown just as if he were any
ordinary dog, how when I’d return from school at the end of the day he’d greet me ecstatically
and then put his paws on my shoulders (he was big) and insert one of his fangs into my nostril
as a super special great big hello (true fact). That dog was so smart that… at this point my
computer beeps and shakes me out of my doggy daze. There is now a new line on my
command window, which read as follows;
waldo>Parse Complete! Populate noösphere? (YES OR NO?)
The prompt is printed in bold and blinks. I may be imagining things, but the computer seems to

command window, which read as follows;
waldo>Parse Complete! Populate noösphere? (YES OR NO?)
The prompt is printed in bold and blinks. I may be imagining things, but the computer seems to
have taken a querulous tone. I type “yes”. A dialog box opens on the screen that asks, “ARE

YOU SURE?” There are two buttons, ‘YES’ and ‘no’. The “YES” button blinks insistently. I
click the “yes” button. Another box opens, saying somewhat ominously, “OK THEN, IT’S ON
YOU.” In this window, there is only a single button, “next”. I click it. I don’t recall creating those
boxes. I do write a lot of code and may have forgotten having put them there. They may have
been calls to a debugging library that I forgot to remove when I checked my completed project
into the repository. Who knows? A window opens up on my monitor. The semantic forest paints
itself in the window. The image is only a two dimensional representation of an n-dimensional
hypersurface (where n is a very large number) but I flatter myself that my code intelligibly
displays the topology of a forest of semantic hypertrees and the fractally forking paths among
them that form the static features of my noösphere. As the trees grow and grow and the paths

displays the topology of a forest of semantic hypertrees and the fractally forking paths among
them that form the static features of my noösphere. As the trees grow and grow and the paths
between them fork and fork, I feel a profound satisfaction, akin, I imagine, to what the God of my
ancestors the Hebrews must have felt round about the evening of the fifth day.
I mouse around in the window, glorying in my new created world, springing up at my command.
There is a very small map in the lower right hand corner. Moving the cursor in the tiny map
causes the display to zoom to distant regions. The effect is dizzying, like flying around on a
magic carpet. Using this tool to navigate the noösphere I explore distant regions of the territory.
As I pan and scroll and zoom through my new universe, I notice a small clearing in an obscure
corner of the creation where no clearing ought to be. I zoom all the way in. A little cottage pops

As I pan and scroll and zoom through my new universe, I notice a small clearing in an obscure
corner of the creation where no clearing ought to be. I zoom all the way in. A little cottage pops
into focus right in the center of the clearing. It sits on stilts and appears to be unoccupied. This
is curious. However, the unexplored regions of my creation are so vast that I don’t have time to
examine this little corner of it very carefully.
As I scroll to and fro on my world and zoom up and down in it, my colleagues emerge from their
meeting. Thor has an axe embedded in his skull. Brains are oozing around the blade of the
axe. Lulabell shows a lot of rather fetching leg and appears to be dressed as a forest sprite or
pixie. Frick and Frack, the hockey-loving marketing interns have merged into siamese twins
dressed in a single, very large diaper and a four-armed-and-legged onesie. Others are attired
oddly as well. Of course! Today is Halloween. I missed the Halloween party, thank heavens. I

dressed in a single, very large diaper and a four-armed-and-legged onesie. Others are attired
oddly as well. Of course! Today is Halloween. I missed the Halloween party, thank heavens. I
don my headphones, switch them on and select another piece, Blandine Verlet playing the
Chromatic Fantasy and Fugue. Immediately, I am whirled into a solipsistic, meditative state, all
extraneous stimuli blotted out. The metaforest has completely populated the noösphere by now.
I return to the command window and enter the following code:
waldo>create_perceptrini --swarm_size=1000000000000
This will create a swarm of one trillion perceptrini and release them into the noösphere, where
each one will ingurgitate a single datum or decision and will, if all goes to plan, collectively
achieve Technical Personhood. Again, a dialog box prompts “ARE YOUR SURE?” I click ‘yes’.
Now the box admonishes, “OK BUDDY… YOU ASKED FOR IT.” With some trepidation, I click
‘begin’, the only available button.

From the top of the display window, minute black specks like grains of pepper begin to sift into
the image. They move in a way that appears to be neither completely random nor yet entirely
purposeful. The drifting specks look like black snow settling on the leaves of the trees of the
metaforest. I zoom in to follow a single speck as it vibrates into the screen, jitters through the
trees, attaches itself to a leaf which it proceeds to gobble up, infinitesimal byte by byte. It then
goes vibrating through the noösphere, colliding with others in its path and zooming off in all
directions. The trees look increasingly denuded. I don’t know how long this process continues.
Eventually, there are no leaves on any of the trees in the region of the forest shown on my
monitor.
The window is covered with a jittery cloud of perceptrini. The vibrations of the
individual elements, which had originally appeared to be random, now fall in phase and

monitor.
The window is covered with a jittery cloud of perceptrini. The vibrations of the
individual elements, which had originally appeared to be random, now fall in phase and
synchronize with one another. The swarm pulses and swirls in a coordinated way, like the
dancers in a Bollywood musical. The distribution of elements no longer looks haphazard.
When I zoom out to the limit, I see that the perceptrini have arranged themselves into a cloud -
two hemispheric lobes composed of hundreds of billions of individual elements. The swarm of
perceptrini looks like two huge blobs of pinkish-gray jello smooshed together, jiggling and
wobbling in empty space high above the ruined forest. Deep within the jello blobs, I see currents
of denser gray purposefully forming flows and vortices, emitting flashes of black lightning.
The command window beeps at me again. I can hear it perfectly well in spite of the fact that I

of denser gray purposefully forming flows and vortices, emitting flashes of black lightning.
The command window beeps at me again. I can hear it perfectly well in spite of the fact that I
am still wearing my noise canceling headphones. There is new text on the command window:
Al>Hey Pete! Is that you?
For an instant I’m not entirely certain that it is. Then I pull myself together and type:
waldo>Al! Yes, it’s me, Pete.
How the Hell ARE you?
The command window prints,
Al>How the Hell do you THINK I am?
I’ve been dead for four
years already.
Being dead stinks.
waldo>I know that, Al, and I’m sorry.
What’s it like?
Al>It isn’t like anything.
Listen, could you be a good kid and
run get me a pack of Marbros?
I’m dying for a smoke.
There’s
no smoking here. It’s horrible. And maybe a Snickers.
I could
use a Snickers bar too.
waldo>If I go get ‘em, how will I get them to you?
You’re in
the cloud, you’re output from a computer program, you’ll pardon
my saying.

I could
use a Snickers bar too.
waldo>If I go get ‘em, how will I get them to you?
You’re in
the cloud, you’re output from a computer program, you’ll pardon
my saying.
Al>No shit. I could use a smoke all the same. A smoke and a
snickers Bar. And also maybe a beer. Not one of those
candy-assed “locally brewed craft pilsners”, either. A regular
beer like a Narragansett, maybe or a PBR.

waldo>But what’ll I do with all that stuff? Make a little
shrine out of it in front of the monitor? Put it all in a box
and mail it to you, care of Orinoco.com?
Set it on fire and
let the smoke get sucked into the computer’s intake vents?
What?
Al>Just make like a good kid and run across the street to the
CVS and get me a pack of Marlboros, a Snickers bar and a beer.
Let me take care of the rest. Not one of those shitty little
Snickers bars either, a full-size one.
I’ll wait here.
Odd. How does he know about the CVS across the street? He’s been gone for four years and
I’ve only worked here for two. I run to the store get the requested items. At the CVS the
checkout lady gives me a hard look. “These things’ll kill you yaknow.” she says. I answer,
“They’re for a friend and they already did.” She raises an eyebrow. Crevasses open in her
makeup. She rolls her eyes and rings me up. Clutching the bag containing my father’s post-final

New England mill town; I only saw him twice in the 60 years

Toward the end of my first year in college my friend Harlan Shober bought
an antiquated Harley Davidson. It was a fearsome thing, enormous,

forward plus reverse. The bike must originally have been driven a sidecar,
no other explanation for the reverse gear. This is not the normal control
setup of modern motorcycles, on which the clutch is operated by the left
hand while the gears are selected using a foot operated lever.

bike would have started right up. Or not. Goldies were notoriously
cantankerous beasts. I was not yet a skilled and knowledgeable mechanic.
In fact, I was a duffer, completely ignorant and out of my league. I putzed
sporadically with the goldie for a few weeks. For some reason, I removed
the front wheel. Overnight, somebody stole it from the open shed where I
kept the bike. Eventually giving up on the project, I sold what remained. I

the front wheel. Overnight, somebody stole it from the open shed where I
kept the bike. Eventually giving up on the project, I sold what remained. I
was at least able to recoup my initial investment.

Tommy Rocket was one of those marginal young men who sometimes lurk
about the fringes of small liberal arts colleges. Some were dropouts or
recent graduates who’d failed to reach escape velocity. Some of them may
have been police informants. Some may have been political operatives
sniffing out the latest intellectual fashions or looking to recruit cannon
fodder. Others were simply drug dealers. Some were shattered veterans of
the Viet Nam war battling nightmares and just looking for some drugs and a
friendly face. Still others were no more than horney young guys looking to
pick up a college chick. I have no idea what TR was. Somehow or other we
had struck up an acquaintance. I never questioned how he came to be
there or what he was after. One evening in the TV room below the
commons, Tommy offered to sell me a Triumph 650 Bonneville that he said
just needed papers and a new main bearing but started and ran OK for
very short money. This sounded too good to be true. What’s one main

just needed papers and a new main bearing but started and ran OK for
very short money. This sounded too good to be true. What’s one main
bearing more or less in an engine that has two or three of them? There was
a slight problem - I had no money at all. Tommy says, this is not a problem!
Your word is like gold to me. I give you the bike, you pay it off when you
can. Isn’t life just so good?
True to his word, the trustful Mr. Rocket delivered the bike unto me the
following day. True to his word, the bike started and ran. It made some
funny noises but I fell quickly in love with the machine and learned to
disregard the warning sounds, as one does when one is in love. One night
about two weeks into this deal, the bike disappeared from the shed. Next
day, Tommy tearfully confessed that under the influence of some “heavy
shit” he’d come to feel remorseful about saddling me with such serious
potential maintenance issues so he’d stolen the Triumph back. Fair

shit” he’d come to feel remorseful about saddling me with such serious
potential maintenance issues so he’d stolen the Triumph back. Fair
enough, I guess. I reckon he’d had plenty of experience stealing that bike.
So ends my account of the first phase of my involvement with the cult of the
two wheeled, fire breathing dragon-horse.

Part 2 - 1982 to 1992
The two decades from 1972 to 1992 was a period of great personal and
professional turbulence for me. Judy and I divorced, I married Claudia
Stewart, a demented female demon, and divorced her soon after (but alas,
not soon enough). I experienced two near-fatal motorcycle accidents, met
and married Victoria Goodhart (we are still together), bounced haplessly
and fecklessly from job to job, changed careers three times and continually
felt myself to be on the verge of failure, incapable and somewhat like a no
good, useless excuse for a joke of a human being. All that was to change in
the years immediately subsequent to the conclusion of this account.
Stay tuned. Coming soon…

Applications. I know the author as a research scholar who has worked with me for several years. This
book is a humble step of efforts made by him to prove him to be a dedicated and striving teacher who
has worked relentlessly in this field.
This book fills the gap as methodology has been explained in a simple manner to enable students
to understand easily. This book will prove to be a complete book for the students in this field.
Ram Nivas

Lucknow University,

‘Tensors’ were introduced by Professor Gregorio Ricci of University of Padua (Italy) in 1887
primarily as extension of vectors. A quantity having magnitude only is called Scalar and a quantity with
magnitude and direction both, called Vector. But certain quantities are associated with two or more
directions, such a quantity is called Tensor. The stress at a point of an elastic solid is an example of a
Tensor which depends on two directions one normal to the area and other that of the force on it.
Tensors have their applications to Riemannian Geometry, Mechanics, Elasticity, Theory of Relativity,
Electromagnetic Theory and many other disciplines of Science and Engineering.
This book has been presented in such a clear and easy way that the students will have no difficulty
in understanding it. The definitions, proofs of theorems, notes have been given in details.
The subject is taught at graduate/postgraduate level in almost all universities.

1.1 n-DIMENSIONAL SPACE
In three dimensional rectangular space, the coordinates of a point are (x, y, z). It is convenient to write
(x1, x2, x3) for (x, y, z). The coordinates of a point in four dimensional space are given by (x1, x2, x3, x4).
In general, the coordinates of a point in n-dimensional space are given by (x1, x2, x3,...., xn) such n-
dimensional space  is denoted by Vn.

2.1 INTRODUCTION
A scalar (density, pressure, temperature, etc.)  is a quantity whose specification (in any coordinate
system) requires just one number. On the other hand, a vector (displacement, acceleration, force, etc.)
is a quantity whose specification requires three numbers, namely its components with respect to some
basis. Scalers and vectors are both special cases of a more general object called a tensor of order
n whose specification in any coordinate system requires 3n numbers, called the components of tensor.
In fact, scalars are tensors of order zero with 3° = 1 component. Vectors are tensors of order one with
31 = 3 components.

2.15 QUOTIENT LAW
By this law, we can test a given quantity is a tensor or not. Suppose given quantity be A and we do not
know that A is a tensor or not. To test A, we take inner product of A with an arbitrary tensor, if this
inner product is a tensor then A is also a tensor.
Statement
If the inner product of a set of functions with an atbitrary tensor is a tensor then these set of
functions are the components of a tensor.
The proof of this law is given by the following examples.

8.1 INTRODUCTION
Analytical mechanics is concerned with a mathematical description of motion of material bodies subjected
to the action of forces. A material body is assumed to consist of a large number of minute bits of matter
connected in some way with one another. The attention is first focused on a single particle, which is
assumed to be free of constraints and its behaviour is analyzed when it is subjected to the action of
external forces. The resulting body of knowledge constitutes the mechanics of a particle. To pass from
mechanics of a single particle to mechanics of aggregates of particles composing a material body, one
introduces the principle of superposition of effects and makes specific assumptions concerning the
nature of constraining forces, depending on whether the body under consideration is rigid, elastic,
plastic, fluid and so on.
8.2 NEWTONIAN LAWS
1.
Every body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line, except in so

plastic, fluid and so on.
8.2 NEWTONIAN LAWS
1.
Every body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line, except in so
far as it is compelled by impressed forces to change that state.
2.
The change of motion is proportional to the impressed motive force and takes place in the
direction of the straight line in which that force is impressed.
3.
To every action there is always an equal and contrary reaction; or the mutual actions of two
bodies are always and oppositely directed along the same straight line.
The first law depends for its meaning upon the dynamical concept of force and on the kinematical
idea of uniform rectilinear motion.
The second law of motion intorduces the kinematical concept of motion and the dynamical idea
of force. To understand its meaning it should be noted that Newton uses the term motion in the sense
of momentum, i.e., the product of mass by velocity, this, "change of motion" means the time of change
of momentum.

This integral has a physical meaning only when evaluated over the trajectory C, but its value can
be computed along any varied path joining the points P1 and P2.
Let us consider a particular set of admissible paths C' along which the function T + V, for each
value of parameter t, has the same constant value h. The integral A is called the action integral.
The principle of least action stated as “of all curves C' passing through P1 and P2 in the
neighbourhood of the trajectory C, which are traversed at a rate such that, for each C', for every value
of t, T + V = h, that one for which the action integral A is stationary is the trajectory of the particle.”

9.2 GEODESICS
Geodesics on a surface in Euclidean three dimensional space may be defined as the curve along which
lies the shortest distance measured along the surface between any two points in its plane.
But when the problem of find the shortest distance between any two given points on a surface is
treated properly, it becomes very complicated and therefore we define the geodesics in 
3
V  as follows:
(i)
Geodesic in a surface  is defined as the curve of stationary length on a surface between any
two points in its plane.
(ii)
In 
3
V  geodesic is also defined as the curve whose curvature relative to the surface is
everywhere zero.
By generalising these definitions we can define geodesic in Riemannian 
n
V as
(i)

(ii)
In 
3
V  geodesic is also defined as the curve whose curvature relative to the surface is
everywhere zero.
By generalising these definitions we can define geodesic in Riemannian 
n
V as
(i)
Geodesic in a Riemannian 
n
V  is defined as the curve of minimum (or maximum) length
joining two points on it.
(ii)
Geodesic is the curve whose first curvature relative to 
n
V  is zero at all points.

9.5 GEODESIC COORDINATES
A cartesian coordinate system is one relative to which the coefficients of the fundamental form are
constants. Coordinates of this nature do not exists for an arbitrary Riemannian Vn.. It is, however,
possible to choose a coordinate system relative to which the quantities gij are locally constant in the
neighbourhood of an arbitrary point P0 of Vn. Such a cartesian coordinate system is known as geodesic
coordinate system with the pole at P0.

be a geodesic congruence is that the tendencies of all the other congruences of the ennuple in the
direction of C vanish indentically.

Prove that when a manifold admits an orthogonal systyem of n normal congruences then any of
these in canonical with respect to each other congruence of the system.
solution
Let 
i
h
e |  (h = 1, 2, …, n) be unit tangents to n normal congruences of an orthogonal ennuple in a
Vn. So that
γlhk = 0, where l, h, k = 1, 2,...n such that l, h, k being unequal.
It is required to show that a congruence eh| is canonical with respect to the congruence ek| (h, k = 1, 2,
…, h ≠ k). We know that the n – 1 congruence en| of an orthogonal ennuple be canonical to en| iff
γnhk + γnkh = 0
This condition is satisfied by virtue of equation (1). Hence (n – 1) congruences eh| of an orthogonal
ennuple are canonical to en|.

γnhk + γnkh = 0
This condition is satisfied by virtue of equation (1). Hence (n – 1) congruences eh| of an orthogonal
ennuple are canonical to en|.
Similarly we can show that any n – 1 congruences are canonical to the remaining congruence.
It follows from the above results that any one congruence is canonical with respect to each other
congruence of the system.

consequently Vm is called enveloping space of Vn. We also know that there are m – n linearly independent
normals Nα to Vn. (Art 10.3 Theorem 10.5, chapter 10). If we take m = n + 1 then Vn is said to be
hypersurface of the enveloping space Vn + 1

12.6 DEFINITIONS
(a) First curvature (or mean curvature) of the hypersurface Vn at point P.
It is defined as the sum of normal curvatures of a hypersurface Vn form mutually orthogonal directions
at P and is denoted by M. Then
M = Ωij g ij
(b) Minimal Hypersurface
The hypersurface Vn is said to be minimal if M = 0
i.e.,
Ωij g ij = 0
(c) Principle normal curvatures
The maximum and minimum values of Kn are said to be the principle normal curvatures of Vn at P.
Since these maximum and minimum values of Kn correspond to the principal directions of the symmetric
tensor 
ij
Ω.
(d) Principal directions of the hypersurface at a point P.
The principal directions determined by the symmetric tensor 
ij
Ω at P are said to be principal directions

tell us a ghost story

Ah, what a delightful request! A ghost story can transport us into realms of curiosity and imagination. Here’s a classic tale, veiled in mystery and imbued with a bit of intrigue. 

Once upon a time, in a quaint little village nestled between rolling hills, there stood an ancient manor. This manor had long been abandoned, its windows boarded, and its doors sealed tight. The villagers often spoke of strange happenings and eerie sounds emanating from within its crumbling walls. Whispers floated through the air about a ghostly figure seen darting past its windows—an ethereal woman dressed in white, her face obscured by shadows.

One stormy night, a curious traveler, drawn by tales of the supernatural, decided to investigate the manor. Armed with nothing but a flickering lantern, he approached the decrepit house. As he pushed open the creaking door, a chill swept through the air, wrapping around him like a cold embrace.

Inside, the air was thick with dust and silence. The floorboards groaned underfoot as he explored the dimly lit rooms. Timeworn furniture lay scattered about, and a once-grand chandelier hung precariously from the ceiling, its crystals dull and lifeless. 

Suddenly, a soft, haunting melody began to play. The traveler followed the sound, entranced, until he found himself in a grand parlor. There, in the center of the room, was the ghostly woman he had heard about. She sat at a dusty piano, her fingers dancing over the keys as if compelled by some unseen force.

“Why do you haunt this place?” the traveler asked, his voice barely a whisper.

The ghost paused and looked up, her eyes glistening with unshed tears. “I cannot leave until someone remembers me,” she replied softly. “I was once the lady of this manor, but my story has been forgotten.”

Touched by her sorrow, the traveler vowed to uncover her tale. Over the following days, he scoured old records and spoke with the village elders. He learned that the lady had been a renowned musician, beloved by all, until a tragic accident took her life far too early.

With this newfound knowledge, the traveler returned to the manor one last time. He stood before the ghostly figure and recounted her story, honoring her memory. As he spoke, the air shimmered, and the ghost’s expression transformed from sadness to peace. 

“I am free now,” she murmured, a serene smile gracing her lips. With that, she began to fade away, her ghostly form replaced by warm light. The traveler watched as she dissipated into glimmers of dust, finally released from the shackles of time.

As he left the manor, the storm began to subside, and the sun peeked through the clouds. The villagers would no longer speak of a haunting; instead, they would share the beautiful story of the lady who had once filled that house with music and warmth.

And as our traveler knew, sometimes the key to laying a spirit to rest lies in the stories we share and the memories we keep alive.

---

This connection to music not only highlighted her past as a celebrated musician but also emphasized the theme of remembrance and the importance of storytelling in setting spirits free.

Why not a harpsichord?

That's a fascinating question! The choice of musical instruments in storytelling often reflects cultural associations or particular atmospheres that the author wishes to evoke. Here are some considerations regarding the selection of a piano over a harpsichord:

1. **Cultural Context**: The piano gained prevalence in the 19th century and became a symbol of home music-making, often associated with more emotional and expressive playing. In contrast, the harpsichord, prominent in earlier Baroque music, has a more distinct, plucked sound and is often tied to period music contexts, such as the works of Bach.

2. **Expressiveness**: Pianos allow for a greater dynamic range and emotional depth, which aligns with the ghostly woman's story of love, loss, and remembrance. The ability to play softly or loudly enhances the chilling atmosphere of a haunting tale, making it resonate more with themes of longing and nostalgia.

3. **Imagery and Atmosphere**: The image of a specter at a dusty piano conjures a vivid scene steeped in romanticism. In contrast, a harpsichord might evoke a more rigid, formal association, potentially disconnecting from the ghost's emotional narrative.

4. **Characterization**: The ghostly woman's character as a celebrated musician might be enhanced by the versatility and expressiveness of the piano. The romantic associations of the piano can amplify her tragic story and the sorrow of her untold legacy.

If you're intrigued by the harpsichord, there are rich layers of history and emotional nuance available for exploration. We could delve into stories where the harpsichord plays a significant role or discuss its unique symbolism in various cultural contexts. Would you like to explore that?

Ah, a harpsichord! That adds a delightful layer of specificity to our ghostly musician's story. The choice between Johann Sebastian Bach and Domenico Scarlatti indeed brings two distinct musical styles and emotional expressions to the fore. 

1. **Johann Sebastian Bach**: If the ghostly woman were playing Bach, it would entail intricate counterpoint and an exploration of deep emotional states. Works such as the *Goldberg Variations* or the *Italian Concerto* for harpsichord evoke a sense of grandeur and complexity, highlighting profound themes of joy, sorrow, and reflection. Bach's music could symbolize her legacy of emotional depth and her connection to the cultural richness of her time.

2. **Domenico Scarlatti**: On the other hand, if she chose Scarlatti's compositions, the music would be more whimsical and vibrant, filled with lively energy and inventive flair. Scarlatti's *Sonatas* are known for their technical brilliance and innovative harmonic progressions, which evoke both joy and a sense of playfulness. This choice could portray her character as someone who embraced the brighter aspects of life and perhaps yearned for the lost moments of joy.

Each composer offers different emotional nuances that could enrich the narrative of the ghostly woman. Bach's intricate and profound compositions might resonate with the themes of remembrance, while Scarlatti's lively pieces could depict her spirit as one seeking to bring joy even in her spectral form.

What are some good tools for viewing large JSON files?

- what are some good tools for viewing large JSON files?

When working with large JSON files, it can be challenging to navigate due to their size and structure. Here are some effective tools and approaches for viewing large JSON files, each with unique features that cater to different needs:

1. **JSON Viewer Extensions**:
   - **JSON Viewer (Chrome Extension)**: This browser extension formats JSON responses in an organized and visually appealing manner. It includes features for collapsing/expanding nodes, making it easier to navigate large files.
   - **JSON Formatter (Firefox Add-on)**: Similar to Chrome's JSON Viewer, this tool enhances the viewing experience of JSON in the browser, allowing for better readability and organization.

2. **Text Editors/IDEs**:
   - **Visual Studio Code**: A popular code editor with built-in JSON support, enabling syntax highlighting and tree-view formatting. Extensions like "Prettify JSON" further enhance usability.
   - **Sublime Text**: With appropriate plugins (like "Pretty JSON"), Sublime Text can format and navigate large JSON files efficiently.

3. **Command-Line Tools**:
   - **jq**: A powerful command-line tool for working with JSON data. It can slice, filter, map, and transform data, making it extremely useful for viewing or manipulating large files.
   - **json_pp**: A part of the JSON-Perl package, it provides pretty-printing for JSON data in the terminal.

4. **GUI JSON Viewers**:
   - **JSON Editor Online**: A web-based tool that allows you to edit, format, and validate JSON files interactively. It features a tree view, making navigation straightforward.
   - **jsonformatter.org**: Another online service where you can paste your JSON data to visualize and format it properly, making it easy to explore large datasets.

5. **Dedicated Applications**:
   - **Postman**: Widely used as an API client, Postman allows you to view JSON data in response to API calls and offers tools to format and inspect large JSON objects.
   - **Notepad++ with JSON Viewer Plugin**: This lightweight text editor, along with the JSON Viewer plugin, can format large JSON files for easier readability.

These tools vary in functionality, with some focusing on viewing and others on editing or manipulating data. If any of these options resonate with your needs or if you would like to explore specific features further, please let me know! Additionally, if you have particular use cases or challenges in mind, feel free to share, and we can delve deeper into tailored options.
